mappings.rationaleAI: Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet is a foundational tragedy (C.3.a) whose embedded sonnets and poetic language exemplify prosodic style in drama (B.2.b), and it permanently shaped Western literary conceptions of romantic love (D.1).

Traces the pastoral as a literary genre (A.6) from Theocritus through Virgil to Elizabethan poetry, encompassing pastoral lyric (C.4.b) and pastoral romance (C.2.c) across the Western literary tradition (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Songs of Innocence and of Experience
mappings.rationaleAI: Blake's complementary poetry collections exemplify the lyric form (C.4) and the distinction between verse and prose (C.1), while their integration of relief-etched visual art with poetry illustrates literature's integration with other arts (A.5).

Bronte's Jane Eyre is a landmark in the development of the English novel (C.2.d), blending Gothic romance elements (C.2.c) with social realism, and it occupies a central place in Western literary history (D.1).

Borges mastered the short story as an intellectual art form (C.2.e) and produced influential essays and reviews of imaginary books (C.6.a), profoundly shaping postmodern literary theory and Latin American literature within the Western canon (D.1).

Traces the origins and development of literature from the invention of writing systems through cuneiform tablets to classical genres, directly addressing the nature and scope of literature (A.1), its history (D), and the interaction between oral and written traditions (C.8).

Wordsworth and Coleridge's Lyrical Ballads is a founding text of English Romanticism in poetry (C.1) and lyric form (C.4), and Wordsworth's 1800 Preface articulated a theoretical manifesto for poetic composition (A.7).
Carroll's Alice is one of the best-known works of children's literature (C.7), functioning simultaneously as a novel (C.2.d) and literary satire through its play with logic and language (C.5).

Covers lyric poetry (C.4) from Sappho's sung verse with lyric accompaniment (C.4.a) through Roman innovations in love elegy including sonnet-precursor forms (C.4.b), tracing how these shaped Western literary traditions of personal expression (D.1).

Defoe's Robinson Crusoe is recognized as a founding work of English prose fiction (C.2.d), subject to multiple interpretive frameworks including colonial, religious, and capitalist readings, central to the history of the Western novel (D.1).

Directly examines the epic as a literary genre (C.2.a, A.6) from Homer and Virgil through Chaucer and Milton, including the relationship between epic and the novel as successor form, spanning the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Examines biography as a literary genre (C.6.o) within nonfictional prose literature (C.6), addressing the biographer's role as narrator, questions of truth versus invention, and the ethics of representing real lives.

Traces metaphor as a figure of speech (B.1.a) from ancient Mesopotamian poetry through Homeric similes, medieval allegory, Metaphysical conceits, and Modernist experimentation, directly addressing rhetoric (B.1) and literary composition techniques (A.2).

Shakespeare's Plantagenet history plays are dramatic literature (C.3) that function as history rendered through literary form (C.6.b), shaping lasting popular perceptions of medieval English kings within the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Samuel Johnson's Lives of the Poets and Shakespeare edition represent landmark literary criticism (A.7, C.6.c), while his Dictionary standardized English literary language, making him central to 18th-century Western literary culture (D.1).

Milton's Paradise Lost is a supreme literary epic (C.2.a) and major achievement in English poetry (C.1), while Areopagitica stands as landmark political literature (C.6.f), together making Milton a towering figure in Western literary history (D.1).

Donne, Marvell, and Herbert exemplify lyric poetry (C.1, C.4.b) distinguished by elaborate conceits and metaphorical wit (B.1.a), and T.S. Eliot's critical revival of their work shaped 20th-century literary sensibility (D.1).

Aristophanes and Menander represent the origins of Western comedy as dramatic literature (C.3.b), combining political satire (C.5) with theatrical innovation at the foundation of the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Pushkin's Eugene Onegin is a verse novel combining the novel form (C.2.d) with poetry (C.1), a masterpiece of Russian Romantic literature examining the superfluous man archetype within the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Fielding's Tom Jones is one of the most influential early English novels (C.2.d), blending comic genius and social satire (C.5) with epic ambition, central to the development of the novel form in Western literature (D.1).

Traces the Gothic as a literary genre (A.6) from Walpole's Castle of Otranto through Radcliffe, the Shelleys, and the Brontes, encompassing Gothic romance (C.2.c) and the Gothic novel (C.2.d) as anti-Enlightenment literary movements (D.1).

Medieval Icelandic prose narratives are the definitive examples of saga literature (C.2.b), blending oral tradition with written literary form (C.8), and later influenced major Western writers including Scott, Morris, and Auden (D.1).

Virgil's Aeneid is the foundational literary epic of Roman civilization (C.2.a) and a supreme achievement of Western poetry (C.1), shaping Latin literary tradition, medieval allegory, and European epic for two millennia (D.1).

The Thousand and One Nights is a major collection of short stories and fables (C.2.e, C.2.f) rooted in oral storytelling traditions (C.8), whose cross-cultural transmission from Arabic to European audiences illustrates literature and its audience across civilizations (A.4).

Cranmer's Book of Common Prayer functions as doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d) whose prose style exercised lasting influence on English literary language and devotional writing within Western literature (D.1).

The Mabinogion comprises medieval Welsh prose tales combining Arthurian romance (C.2.c) with legendary saga elements (C.2.b), transmitted through oral tradition (C.8), and brought into the Western literary canon through 19th-century translation (D.1).

Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People is a landmark of history as literature (C.6.b), combining rigorous source criticism with theological narrative and establishing a foundational work of Western literary history (D.1).

Aesop's fables are the archetypal examples of fable as a literary genre (C.2.f), combining moral instruction with entertainment in a tradition transmitted across centuries of Western literature from popular and folk origins (C.8).

The Poetics is the first known work of literary theory (A.7), providing foundational analysis of tragedy and catharsis (C.3.a) that has influenced dramatists and storytellers from the Renaissance to Hollywood within the Western tradition (D.1).

The chivalric code gave rise to courtly love and romance literature (C.2.c), providing the subject matter (A.3) for an entire literary tradition of Arthurian and chivalric romance across medieval European literature.

Dante's Inferno is a supreme narrative poem (C.1) combining epic scope (C.2.a) with political and theological allegory (C.2.f), and stands as one of the greatest works in the Western literary canon (D.1).

The Epic of Gilgamesh is the oldest surviving great work of literature, an ancient Mesopotamian epic (C.2.a) originating in oral and cuneiform traditions (C.8), foundational to the history of literary narrative (D).
Shelley's Frankenstein is a Gothic novel (C.2.c, C.2.d) that helped originate science fiction as a literary genre, occupying a central place in Western Romantic literature (D.1).

De Stael developed foundational ideas behind Romanticism in literary criticism (A.7), authored novels and plays (C.2.d), and bridged French and German literary traditions, influencing comparative literature across early 19th-century Europe (D.1).

Shakespeare's Roman plays including Julius Caesar and Antony and Cleopatra are major dramatic literature (C.3) including tragedies (C.3.a) that used Plutarch as source material and explored political themes through literary imagination (D.1).

Journey to the West is one of China's greatest novels (C.2.d), blending historical pilgrimage narrative with mythological allegory (C.2.f) and drawing on a thousand years of oral and literary retelling (C.8).
Alcott's Little Women is a landmark novel (C.2.d) credited with originating the young adult fiction genre (C.7), situated within 19th-century American literature and women's writing traditions (D.1).

The Heptameron is a major collection of 72 short stories modeled on Boccaccio's Decameron (C.2.e), connecting early modern French literary production with Renaissance humanism in Western literary history (D.1).

Ovid's Metamorphoses is a sweeping Latin epic poem (C.2.a, C.1) whose tales of transformation influenced Chaucer, Shakespeare, and Ted Hughes, making it one of the most enduring works in the Western literary tradition (D.1).
Moliere is France's greatest comic playwright (C.3.b), whose works including Tartuffe and Le Misanthrope blend farce, social satire (C.5), and moral insight, shaping European comic drama (D.1).

Graves produced both the historical novel I, Claudius (C.2.d) and distinguished poetry (C.1), along with the war memoir Goodbye to All That (C.6.o), spanning multiple literary forms in 20th-century Western literature (D.1).

One of the four great classical Chinese novels (C.2.d), Romance of the Three Kingdoms blends historical record with literary invention in a romance of warfare and political strategy (C.2.c), with cultural influence across East Asia compared to Homer's impact on the West.

Directly examines literary criticism (A.7, C.6.c) through Shakespeare's centrality, covering debates between formalist, historicist, and postcolonial approaches and questions of canonicity in Western literary culture (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
mappings.rationaleAI: A late medieval alliterative poem (C.1) combining Arthurian romance (C.2.c) with sophisticated prosodic technique (B.2), significant for the study of Middle English literature within the Western tradition (D.1).

Sturm und Drang was a pivotal German literary movement (A.6) producing Goethe's Werther (C.2.d) and Schiller's dramatic works (C.3), marking the transition from Enlightenment classicism to Romanticism in Western literary history (D.1).

Li Bai and Du Fu represent the classical pinnacle of Chinese poetry (C.1), with Li Bai's personal lyrics (C.4) and Du Fu's historical verse exemplifying regulated verse forms perfected during the Tang dynasty.

The Grimm fairy tales represent folklore preservation as literary art (C.8), functioning as both children's literature (C.7) and fable (C.2.f), blending horror, wonder, and moral instruction from Germanic oral tradition.

The Devil as literary subject matter (A.3) features centrally in Dante's epic depiction of Satan and Milton's Paradise Lost (C.2.a), with Blake's reading of Milton illustrating allegory and theological narrative as literary modes (C.2.f).

The Ramayana is one of the greatest epic poems in world literature (C.2.a), composed in Sanskrit verse (C.1), comparable in stature to Homer's Iliad and Odyssey and foundational to Indian literary tradition.

Woolf's A Room of One's Own is a landmark essay (C.6.a) that functions as foundational literary criticism (A.7) on women and literature, with enduring influence on feminist literary studies in the Western tradition (D.1).

Cervantes' Don Quixote is arguably the first European novel (C.2.d), parodying chivalric romance (C.2.c, C.5), and its 400-year influence on Sterne, Goethe, Flaubert, Dostoevsky, and Kafka makes it foundational to Western literary history (D.1).
Shakespeare's comedy A Midsummer Night's Dream is a major work of dramatic literature (C.3.b Comedy), combining verse and prose (C.1 Poetry), and a landmark of Western literary history (D.1).

The Agincourt Carol and Shakespeare's Henry V are discussed as cultural legacy; the Carol is a music-based lyric celebrating the victory (C.4.a), and the battle's literary commemoration spans poetry and dramatic literature (C.1).

Antarah's odes are major works of lyric poetry (C.4.b language-based lyrics such as odes), his saga of Antar and Abla is a narrative epic tradition (C.2.a Epic), and his work belongs to the history of non-Western literature (D).
Antigone is one of the greatest works of tragic dramatic literature (C.3.a Tragedy) and a central text in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Behn's novel Oroonoko is an early English novel (C.2.d), she wrote comedies for the Restoration stage (C.3.b), produced poetry (C.4.b), and is a significant figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).
WH Auden is a major 20th-century English-language poet, with his work representing language-based lyric poetry (C.4.b) and a significant chapter in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Aurora Leigh is an 11,000-line epic poem (C.2.a Epic, specifically the 'literary' epic), a major work of Victorian poetry (C.1), and a landmark in the history of Western literature (D.1) addressing gender and social reform.
The Brus is a 14,000-line epic poem in rhyming couplets (C.2.a Epic), the earliest surviving poem in Older Scots (C.1 Poetry), and a landmark of medieval Scottish literary history (D.1).
Disraeli's novels including Sybil or The Two Nations are significant Victorian novels (C.2.d the novel) with a political dimension (C.6.f Political literature).

Beowulf is the foundational Anglo-Saxon epic poem (C.2.a Epic), drawing on folk and popular literary traditions (C.8 Primitive, folk, and popular literature), and a cornerstone of the history of Western literature (D.1).
Brecht's plays span tragedy and comedy in dramatic literature (C.3.a, C.3.b), and he is a major figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).
Camus authored novels (C.2.d the novel and the tale), philosophical essays (C.6.e Philosophical literature), and is a major figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Catullus is one of the great Roman lyric poets, pioneering a new way of writing love poetry (C.4.b language-based lyrics), and his influence on Virgil, Ovid, Petrarch, and Elizabethan poets makes him central to the history of Western literature (D.1).

Caxton's commercially viable publication of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales and other popular works transformed the relationship between literature and its audience (A.4) and is a turning point in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Chaucer's Canterbury Tales uses the frame narrative of romance and tale traditions (C.2.c Romance, C.2.d the novel and the tale), Troilus and Criseyde is a major poem (C.1), the Tales contain satiric elements (C.5), and Chaucer is the 'father of English literature' (D.1 history of Western literature).

Chekhov is one of the greatest short story writers (C.2.e the short story), his plays The Cherry Orchard and Three Sisters are major dramatic literature (C.3.a Tragedy), and he is a central figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Byron's Childe Harold's Pilgrimage is a major long poem (C.1) in the romantic epic tradition (C.2.a) that functions as autobiographical literature blending the poet's life with his protagonist (C.6.o).

Christina Rossetti was a major Victorian poet working in lyric forms including sonnets and devotional verse (C.1, C.4.b), her religious poetry constitutes doctrinal literature (C.6.d), and her work belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).
Christine de Pizan wrote The Book of the City of Ladies, a major work of narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d) using allegory to defend women (C.6.f), within the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Colette's Claudine stories and other novels are works of narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d), her technique of inserting herself into her works constitutes autobiographical literature (C.6.o), and she belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).
Dostoevsky's Crime and Punishment is a landmark novel in Western literature (C.2.d, D.1), exploring psychological depth and moral philosophy through the narrative of Raskolnikov's crime and guilt.

Thomas Mann's Death in Venice is a landmark novella (C.2.e short story/antecedents) and work of narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d) in the Western literary tradition (D.1), exploring themes of aestheticism, repression, and artistic decline.
Evelyn Waugh's Decline and Fall is a comic novel (C.2.d) and work of satiric literature (C.5) within the Western literary tradition (D.1), offering biting social satire of 1920s Britain.
The Philippics are masterworks of rhetoric (B.1.a) and oration (C.6.n), establishing the model for fierce political attacks that Cicero later emulated against Mark Antony.
Dylan Thomas was a major lyric poet (C.1, C.4.b), wrote the radio play Under Milk Wood (C.3.g), and his work reviving poetry as performance belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).

Poe wrote Gothic romances and tales of horror (C.2.c), pioneered detective fiction with the novel and tale form (C.2.d), created the short story genre with tales like The Tell-Tale Heart (C.2.e), composed the poem The Raven (C.1), all within Western literature (D.1).
Emily Dickinson is one of the great lyric poets (C.1, C.4.b) of the Western literary tradition (D.1), known for her distinctive voice and exploration of interiority.
Jane Austen's Emma is a landmark novel (C.2.d) in the Western literary tradition (D.1), a comedy of manners that advanced the art of the novel through social observation and irony.

The episode directly examines epistolary literature as a literary form (C.6.m), covering landmark epistolary novels like Richardson's Clarissa (C.2.d), within the development of Western literature (D.1).
In Praise of Folly is a masterwork of satiric literature (C.5) in the Western literary tradition (D.1).

Fanny Burney's Evelina and subsequent novels are landmark works of narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d) in the Western literary tradition (D.1), with Virginia Woolf crediting her as 'the mother of English fiction.'

The Faust myth operates as fable, parable, and allegory (C.2.f), Marlowe's Doctor Faustus is a landmark tragedy in dramatic literature (C.3.a), and Goethe's Faust is a pinnacle of Western literature (D.1).
Fernando Pessoa is among the most significant Western poets since the Middle Ages (D.1), writing lyric poetry (C.1, C.4.b) through distinctive heteronyms each with a unique voice.

TS Eliot's Four Quartets is a major long poem (C.1) using language-based lyric forms (C.4.b), constituting both doctrinal/religious literature (C.6.d) and philosophical literature (C.6.e) in the Western tradition (D.1).
Douglass's 1845 Narrative is a landmark of autobiographical literature (C.6.o), and his powerful oratory exemplifies the oration and speech tradition (C.6.n).

Gerald's Journey Through Wales and Description of Ireland are major examples of medieval travel literature (C.6.l), autobiographical/biographical literature (C.6.o), and history as literature (C.6.b).

Hopkins's odes and sonnets are language-based lyrics (C.4.b); his innovations in sprung rhythm and inscape represent advances in prosodic technique (B.2.a); his place in Victorian/early modernist poetry situates him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Heart of Darkness is a foundational novella in the tradition of the novel and the tale (C.2.d), and a canonical work in the history of Western literature (D.1), pioneering techniques of narrative unreliability.

Dante's Divine Comedy and Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress are major works of allegory and parable (C.2.f) in the history of Western literature (D.1), used here to illustrate theological conceptions of paradise.
The play dramatizes themes of authority, honor, and generational conflict through the tragic and comic genres (C.3.a, C.3.b), and is a landmark in the history of Western literature (D.1).
Achilles and Homeric heroes are examined through epic literature (C.2.a), and the heroic ideal shaped subsequent lyric and poetic traditions (C.4.b).

Horace's Odes are language-based lyrics including odes (C.4.b); the Satires represent satiric literature (C.5); the Ars Poetica and Epistles are essays on literature (C.6.a); his lasting influence places him in the history of Western literature (D.1).
Murdoch's novels including The Bell, The Sea The Sea, and The Black Prince are works of the novel (C.2.d) constituting major contributions to 20th-century Western literature (D.1).

Calvino's novels (Invisible Cities, If On A Winter's Night A Traveller) are experimental fiction within the novel tradition (C.2.d); his collection of Italian folktales connects to folk and popular literature (C.8); his work is central to the history of Western literature (D.1).

Ulysses is a landmark novel (C.2.d) that revolutionized narrative technique through stream of consciousness and stylistic pastiche, employing advanced rhetoric and literary devices (B.1.a), and stands as the defining achievement of literary modernism in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Clare's observational poetry of the English countryside constitutes language-based lyric poetry (C.4.b) and his recognition as among the finest Romantic-era poets places him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Donne's metaphysical poetry (sonnets, elegies) represents language-based lyric forms (C.4.b); his sermons are doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d); he is a major figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Keats's odes (Ode to a Nightingale, Ode on a Grecian Urn, Ode on Melancholy) are canonical examples of language-based lyric poetry (C.4.b), and he is a central figure in the history of Western literature (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
mappings.rationaleAI: A Portrait is a groundbreaking modernist novel in the Bildungsroman tradition (C.2.d), pioneering free indirect discourse and stream of consciousness, and enormously influential on 20th-century Western literature (D.1).

Le Morte d'Arthur is the defining English prose compilation of Arthurian romance (C.2.c on romance: chivalry, adventure, Arthurian romance); it draws on French Arthurian tradition connecting to folk and popular literature (C.8); it is a landmark in the history of Western literature (D.1).
King Lear as dramatic/theatrical literature exemplifies tragedy (C.3.a) and is among the most important works in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Literary modernism transformed the novel (C.2.d) through Joyce and Woolf, and poetry (C.4.b) through Eliot and Pound; the episode engages critical writing about modernism as a movement (A.7) and constitutes a major chapter in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Lorca's Gypsy Ballads represent language-based lyric poetry (C.4.b); his fusion of Andalusian folk traditions and Romani culture connects to folk and popular literature (C.8); he is regarded as Spain's most significant modern poet in the history of Western literature (D.1).
Macbeth exemplifies tragedy as dramatic/theatrical literature (C.3.a) and is a major work in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Madame Bovary is a foundational text of literary realism, pioneering free indirect discourse in the novel tradition (C.2.d), and a landmark in the history of Western literature (D.1) that influenced Zola, James, and the modern novel.

The Book of Margery Kempe is considered the first autobiography in English (C.6.o on autobiographical literature) and describes pilgrimages to Jerusalem, Rome, and Santiago de Compostela, connecting to travel literature (C.6.l).

The episode examines literary representations of masculinity as subject matter in literature (A.3), engages critical and theoretical writing about gender in fiction (A.7), and traces shifts across the history of Western literature (D.1).
The episode examines how stories follow arcs analyzable through mathematical frameworks, engaging literary composition (A.2) and the contents/subject matter of literature (A.3).
Byron, Shelley, and Keats as poets connect to poetry (C.1), language-based lyric forms (C.4.b), and the history of Western literature (D.1).
The Orkneyinga Saga is a medieval Icelandic saga chronicling the Norse Earls of Orkney, directly fitting the saga form (C.2.b) covering king's sagas, legendary sagas, and sagas of Icelanders.
Merlin is a central figure of Arthurian romance (C.2.c covers romance including Arthurian romance), and his legend draws on primitive, folk, and popular literary tradition (C.8).
The sonnet is explicitly listed as a language-based lyric form (C.4.b); the episode covers poetry broadly (C.1) and prosodic elements including metre, stanza, and rhyme (B.2.a).
Tristram Shandy is a novel (C.2.d) that defies emerging conventions of the novel form and anticipated modernist techniques; it belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).

Kipling wrote novels including Kim (C.2.d), was among the earliest to develop the short story as a serious form (C.2.e), composed poetry (C.1), and wrote children's literature including The Jungle Book (C.7).
1984 is a dystopian novel (C.2.d) with strong satiric elements (C.5), central to 20th-century Western literature (D.1).
Shakespeare's works span dramatic literature including tragedy and comedy (C.3), lyric poetry including sonnets (C.4.b), and are central to the history of Western literature (D.1).

The Romantic poets (Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Keats, Byron, Shelley) composed poetry (C.1) including lyric forms (C.4.b), representing a pivotal movement in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Thebes as the setting for more surviving Greek plays than any other location, including tragedies by Sophocles and Euripides (C.3.a -- tragedy), makes it foundational to the history of Western literature (D.1).
The episode addresses the nature and scope of literature (A.1), the contents and subject matter of literature (A.3), and the ethics of autobiographical and memoir literature (C.6.o).

Hazlitt is recognized as the finest English essayist (C.6.a -- the essay), also writing literary criticism as literature (C.6.c) and history as literature (C.6.b); his work belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).
Moby-Dick is a novel (C.2.d) with epic qualities (C.2.a -- the literary epic), central to the history of Western -- specifically American -- literature (D.1).

Wilde's theatrical comedies including The Importance of Being Earnest as comedy (C.3.b), his epigrammatic essays as literary criticism and non-fiction prose (C.5.a), and the relationship between art and ethics in his aestheticism (A.3).
Breton's manifesto and automatic writing as literary techniques involving rhetorical elements (B.1.a); Surrealist poetry by Eluard and Aragon as lyric literature (C.4).
The novel as a literary form (C.2.d the novel and the tale), its nature and scope as literature (A.1), its audience (A.4), and classification of genres (A.6) are the direct subject of this episode.
Hardy's poetry as a body of verse (C.1 poetry), his formally inventive structures combining plain diction with prosodic elements (B.2.a, B.2.b), and his approach to literary composition (A.2).
Tragedy as dramatic or theatrical literature (C.3.a), the classification of tragic works as a literary genre (A.6), and critical and theoretical writings on tragedy from Aristotle onward (A.7).
The Vampyre as a Gothic romance (C.2.c), its role as a novella and tale (C.2.d), and its spawning of penny dreadfuls and short vampire fiction (C.2.e).

The Robin Hood legend originated in medieval ballads (C.2.g ballad, lay, idyll), evolved into romance narratives of chivalry and adventure (C.2.c), and functions as fable and allegory across cultural periods (C.2.f).

The sentimental novel by Sterne, Richardson, and Mackenzie as a distinct novelistic form (C.2.d the novel and the tale), and the literary-philosophical content concerning emotional response and virtue (A.3).

A Modest Proposal as a masterpiece of satirical prose and essay writing (C.5.a), employing sustained irony as a rhetorical figure (B.1.a elements of rhetoric), and functioning as parable and allegory (C.2.f).

The Fisher King legend as Arthurian romance (C.2.c romance of love, chivalry, and adventure; Arthurian romance) and as allegory blending Christian and Celtic symbolism (C.2.f fable, parable, allegory).
The Kalevala as a national literary epic compiled from traditional oral poems (C.2.a epic, the literary epic), drawing on ballad and oral poetic traditions (C.2.g ballad, lay, idyll).
The Prelude as poetry (C.1), an autobiographical epic in blank verse (C.2.a epic, the literary epic), and a landmark in literary composition exploring memory, identity, and nature (A.2).

Middlemarch is a landmark Victorian novel by George Eliot, exemplifying the realist tradition in the novel form (C.2.d). Its place in the history of English literature as a masterwork of psychological realism and social breadth situates it within the history of Western literature (D.1).
Sophocles' Oedipus Rex is a canonical work of dramatic literature in the tragic form (C.3.a), exploring fate versus free will, dramatic irony, and the limits of human knowledge.

Goldsmith wrote across multiple literary genres: the novel The Vicar of Wakefield (C.2.d), the comedy She Stoops to Conquer (C.3.b), and the poem The Deserted Village (C.1). His extraordinary range and place in eighteenth-century English literature situate him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Ovid's Metamorphoses is a vast narrative poem retelling Greek and Roman myths, functioning as literary epic (C.2.a). The Ars Amatoria is a poem of erotic instruction (C.1). Ovid's enduring influence on Western literary tradition places him centrally in the history of literature (D.1).

The episode examines the interrelationship between psychoanalytic theory and literary criticism, including how literary criticism and psychoanalytic interpretation have been mutually constitutive, relating to writings on literature and critical writing (A.7).

Burns's verse in vernacular Scots and his use of dialect and folk tradition exemplify poetry (C.1) and language-based lyric forms (C.4.b). His place in the history of English and Scottish literature situates him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Roman satire as a literary genre invented by Latin writers, spanning Ennius through Juvenal, directly corresponds to satiric literature (C.5). The evolution of satirical techniques across several centuries of Roman literary culture belongs to the history of Western literature (D.1).

Beckett's novels Molloy, Malone Dies, and The Unnamable exemplify the novel form (C.2.d). Waiting for Godot and Endgame are landmark dramatic works in the tragic mode (C.3.a). His central role in postwar modernist literature and the Theatre of the Absurd places him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Sappho's personal lyric poetry addressing love, desire, and beauty using distinctive meters including the Sapphic stanza exemplifies lyric literature (C.4). Her foundational place in Western poetry and centuries of translation and adaptation situate her in the history of literature (D.1).

Silas Marner is George Eliot's most parable-like novel (C.2.f fable/parable/allegory) and also a work of Victorian realist fiction (C.2.d). It translates Christian symbols of redemption into secular and naturalistic terms through narrative fiction.

Browne's Religio Medici, Pseudodoxia Epidemica, and Hydriotaphia are major works of nonfictional prose literature (C.6), spanning doctrinal writing, scientific literature, and philosophical literature. His major contribution to English prose style places him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Wyatt introduced the Petrarchan sonnet into English, adapting it toward the English sonnet structure. The sonnet is a language-based lyric form (C.4.b). His role in transmitting Italian Renaissance poetic forms to English literature places him in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Stevie Smith's poetry, including Not Waving But Drowning, combines dark humor, nursery-rhyme rhythms, and existential themes, exemplifying both poetry (C.1) and lyric literature (C.4). Her distinctive position in mid-century English literature, between light verse and existential despair, places her in the history of literature (D.1).

Thomas Hardy's Tess of the d'Urbervilles is a major Victorian novel exemplifying the realist tradition (C.2.d). Its critiques of class exploitation, sexual double standards, and agrarian decline place it in the history of Western literature (D.1).
The Bacchae is a canonical work of dramatic literature in the tragic form (C.3.a), exploring reason versus irrationality, civic order versus divine chaos.

Mandeville's 1705 poem The Grumbling Hive is a satirical verse allegory (C.1 poetry, C.5 satiric literature), using the fable form to advance economic and moral arguments about the relationship between private vice and public prosperity.

The Holy Grail tradition spans medieval French romance (Chretien de Troyes), Arthurian romance (Malory's Morte d'Arthur), and modern retellings, exemplifying the romance of love, chivalry, and adventure, especially Arthurian romance (C.2.c). Its thousand-year literary history places it in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Homer's Iliad is the foundational literary epic of Western literature (C.2.a), composed within an oral epic tradition. Its exploration of honor, mortality, grief, and the human cost of war established it as a cornerstone of Western literary history (D.1).

The Nibelungenlied is a twelfth-century Middle High German heroic epic drawing on older Norse and Germanic traditions (C.2.a epic, C.2.b saga). Its rediscovery by German Romantics, appropriation by Wagner, and exploitation by Nazi ideology trace its enduring place in the history of Western literature (D.1).

Homer's Odyssey is a foundational literary epic (C.2.a) that established narrative conventions including nonlinear storytelling, psychological depth, and the journey as metaphor. It is a cornerstone of Western literary history (D.1).
The Oresteia is a foundational work of dramatic literature in the tragic form (C.3.a), exploring guilt, retribution, and the transformation of private vendetta into public justice.

Erskine Childers's 1903 novel The Riddle of the Sands is a work of fiction in the novel form (C.2.d), functioning as both gripping thriller and historical document. Its place in the history of English literature and the spy/invasion genre situates it in Western literary history (D.1).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner
mappings.rationaleAI: The Rime of the Ancient Mariner is a narrative poem (C.1 poetry) with ballad characteristics (C.2.g ballad/lay), composed in 1798 as a landmark of English Romantic poetry. Its supernatural imagery, haunting musicality, and exploration of transgression place it in the history of Western literature (D.1).

FitzGerald's free poetic translation of Persian quatrains into English verse, celebrated for sensuous imagery and philosophical scepticism; the Rubaiyat's enormous Victorian readership illustrates literature and its audience (A.4); its prosodic qualities as verse translation relate to prosody (B.2); it is poetry (C.1) and specifically lyric verse akin to ode and elegy forms (C.4.b)

The Scriblerus Club produced major satirical literature including Gulliver's Travels (C.2.d as novel/tale), The Dunciad and mock-heroic works (C.5 as satiric literature); their collaborative satirical project targeting false learning constitutes critical writing about literature (A.7) and exemplifies literature's engagement with intellectual subject matter (A.3)

The Song of Roland is the foundational chanson de geste of French literary tradition, composed for oral performance as verse (C.1 as poetry); it is a literary epic recounting heroic deeds and military valor (C.2.a as epic)

Emma Lazarus's sonnet inscribed on the pedestal, welcoming 'the tired, the poor, and the huddled masses yearning to breathe free,' is a celebrated lyric poem (C.4.b as sonnet) that became one of America's most recognized literary texts

The Tale of Sinuhe is one of the oldest surviving literary narratives, illustrating the nature and scope of literature (A.1); it is narrative imaginative fiction - a novel/tale (C.2.d) or short story antecedent (C.2.e) - set against historical events but almost certainly fictional

The Tempest is a late Shakespeare romance involving adventure, magic, and love on a remote island (C.2.c as romance); it is also dramatic/theatrical literature (C.3) - a play drawing on multiple dramatic forms

Homer's Iliad is the foundational literary epic of Western civilization (C.2.a as epic poetry); it is verse poetry of the highest order (C.1), exploring honour, mortality, and wrath through heroic narrative
Middleton's dramatic output encompasses tragic works like The Changeling and The Revenger's Tragedy (C.3.a as tragedy) and comic works like A Chaste Maid in Cheapside (C.3.b as comedy)

Thoreau's Walden is a masterwork of American literature combining philosophical reflection with nature writing (A.3 as literature's subject matter); his writings on civil disobedience represent prose literature (C.2.d); his literary output shaped American literary tradition

Tristan and Iseult is a foundational medieval romance of love, chivalry, and adventure (C.2.c as romance - specifically the romance of love and adventure); it profoundly influenced courtly love literature and European ideas about romantic love

mappings.episodeTitle: Twelfth Night, or What You Will
mappings.rationaleAI: Twelfth Night is dramatic literature in the comedy genre (C.3.b); its exploration of disguise, mistaken identity, and self-deception through multiple intertwined plots represents Shakespeare's mature comic art

Victorian realism sought to depict ordinary life with fidelity, raising questions about the nature and scope of literature (A.1); Henry James's definition of realism and critical debates about the movement constitute writings on literature (A.7); George Eliot's Middlemarch and Hardy's novels are major examples of the novel form (C.2.d)

The Georgics is a didactic poem in four books treating agriculture, transforming practical instruction into elevated poetry (C.1 as verse/poetry); its hexameter verse and relationship to Hesiod and Lucretius involve prosodic tradition (B.2); its subject matter exploring humanity's relationship with nature and labor (A.3)
Candide is a satirical novella (C.2.d as novel/tale) that is fundamentally satiric literature (C.5); its rapid pace, dark wit, and philosophical sharpness exemplify the art of Enlightenment satire
